title: Chapter 8: The Wound
content: The shift lands soft.
No static. No vertigo. Just the world changing channels like it was always meant to be this one, and everything else was the mistake.
I'm lying on my back in a bed I almost don't recognize, and for three full breaths I don't move. Not because I can't. Because I don't want to break whatever this is.

Hand-knitted blankets — chunky wool in cream and grey, the kind of thing someone made with their own hands over a long winter. The stitches are imperfect. Beautiful because they're imperfect. Rain on the window, steady and slow, the sound of a place that has nowhere urgent to be. My tea is cooling on the nightstand — chamomile, the label curling with steam, a mug with a chipped rim I must have bought at a charity shop because nobody would sell this new. The room smells like lavender and old wood and something baking downstairs, bread maybe, the yeast-sweet kind that lives in the walls of places where someone actually bothers to make bread.
And at the foot of the bed: three cats.

They're arranged in a perfect spiral. Toast — orange, opinionated — at the center. Socks — black with white paws — curled around him like a parentheses. Fog — grey, enormous, more pillow than animal — forming the outermost ring. They're warm against my ankles through the blankets. Their fur is so soft it makes my teeth ache. They're breathing in unison, three small engines of indifference, and they don't care that I just fell out of another reality into this one. They care that it's warm under these blankets and that I haven't moved yet, which means I'm not disturbing them.
This is the version of my life where I chose quiet.

Where I stopped performing. Where I took the exit ramp off the highway of spectacular choices and ended up here — above a café in a seaside town, with cats who don't know my name and tea I let go cold and rain that sounds like forgiveness.
This is the only timeline that feels like home.

I sit up. The cats rearrange themselves in protest — Toast squints at me like I've committed a personal offense, Fog stretches so long he nearly falls off the bed, Socks doesn't move at all because Socks has never acknowledged a problem in his life. I scratch behind Toast's ears. He allows it. Barely.

I pull on a jumper — oversized, cream, sleeves past my fingertips — and pad down the narrow stairs. Each step creaks. The café emerges around me like a place remembering itself: exposed brick, mismatched chairs, the big espresso machine humming on standby. The smell hits me stronger here — fresh bread, yes, and coffee grounds and rain tracked in on the doormat and something green, maybe the basil plant I keep meaning to water.
And Julian.

He's sitting at the counter on one of the stools, a mug of tea between his hands, looking out the window at the rain. The orange cat — the one who's been circling this café like a fur-covered loan shark — is perched on his shoulder like a very judgmental parrot. Its tail flicks against his jaw. He doesn't seem to mind.

His shoulder is wrapped in clean white bandages — I can see the edge of them under the collar of his t-shirt, which is grey and soft and looks like something I might have lent him. He's still pale. Still sharp-jawed and watchful, the kind of person you'd clock as dangerous even in a room full of dangerous people. But the edge is different here. Softer. Like someone took a photograph of a blade and left it in the sun.
He's alive.

The sight of him sitting at my counter, drinking tea from my mugs, with my cat on his shoulder like he belongs there — it does something to my chest. Something I don't have a word for. Something that feels like a door opening onto a room I didn't know the house had.
"You stayed," he says.
Not a question. He doesn't turn to look at me. He keeps his eyes on the rain. Like he's been waiting to say it and now it's out and he doesn't need to make it a big thing.

I walk behind the counter. Pull a mug from the rack — the one with the faded botanical print, the one I always reach for. Pour tea from the pot he must have made. It's still hot. He made enough for two.
"You didn't leave," I say.

Now he looks at me. And there it is — that thing in his eyes that's the same across every version of him I've met. The searching. The part of him that's always scanning for exits, for threats, for the angle. But underneath it, here, there's something else. Something that looks almost like surprise. Like he didn't expect anyone to say that to him, ever.
We drink tea. The rain keeps going. The cat on his shoulder purrs so loudly I can hear it from across the counter — a small motor of approval.
"What's your name?" he asks.

My mouth opens. Hesitates. The thing about names is that they're the truest thing I carry across timelines. My name doesn't change. But saying it here, in this quiet place, to this version of him — it feels like handing over something I can't take back.
"Avery," I say.
He nods. Like it fits. Like he already knew, somehow, and was just being polite by asking.
"Julian," he says. Then, almost as an afterthought: "Most people call me J."
I almost drop my mug.

The ceramic slips between my fingers. I catch it — barely — by pressing it against the counter with my palm. Hot tea sloshes over the rim and burns my skin and I don't care because the sound of that letter in his mouth is ringing through me like a bell struck at the bottom of a well. J. Julian A never offered that. Julian B — I don't know what Julian B offered yet. But this one, this wounded man in my café, he gave it to me like it was nothing. Like it was just a name.
"You okay?" He's frowning at me, at the tea pooling on the counter.
"Fine. Just — clumsy." I grab a cloth, wipe up the mess. My hand is not steady. "J," I add, like I'm testing it. Like I'm tasting it. "Suits you."
He almost smiles. The corner of his mouth. A fraction of a degree.

The morning unfolds. Customers come — a slow trickle of regulars, people who nod at me like I'm part of the furniture, which I apparently am. I make their drinks. I take their money. The rhythm of it is so different from the other timelines — no urgency, no performance, no sense that I'm building toward something. I'm just here. Making lattes. Saying "cheers" when they pay. Wiping down tables that will be wiped down again tomorrow.
And Julian stays.

He doesn't leave. He doesn't explain why he's still here, three days after I stitched his shoulder together with a sewing kit and a bottle of whiskey and hands that were somehow steady even though I was shaking inside. He just stays. And when the first lull hits — between the early crowd and the lunch rush — he starts doing things.

He fixes the wobbly table by the window. Not with tools. With a folded napkin and some clever wedge-work that I watch from three feet away and still don't fully understand. He's down on one knee, his bad shoulder braced against the table leg, and he's concentrating so hard his tongue touches his bottom lip. The cat — who has not left his shoulder this entire time — watches his work with the detached interest of a site inspector.

Then he moves behind the counter. I don't even have time to object before he's at the espresso machine, pulling shots with the muscle memory of someone who's done it for years. The espresso comes out perfect — rich, dark, with crema like burnt gold. I stare at it. I've been making espresso here for I don't know how long and I've never made it look that easy.
"I bar-backed for two years," he says, catching my look. "Before."
Before. I don't ask before what. I already know the answer is complicated.

A woman comes in — fifties, raincoat, argumentative about the weather — and Julian says something dry and unexpected about the seagulls staging a coup, and she laughs. Actually laughs. The sound rings off the brick walls like wind chimes. She looks at him, then at me, and there's a knowing in her eyes that makes my face warm.

The café transforms with him in it. Not dramatically. Not like a renovation. More like someone opened a window that's always been painted shut. The light changes. The sounds change. It goes from being a hiding place — my hiding place, where I've been lurking and surviving and calling it enough — to being a place someone might actually want to stay. A place with warmth in it. A place with someone who fixes things and makes things and makes people laugh while a cat sits on his shoulder like a tiny, fur-covered judge of character.
I watch him from behind the espresso machine. He's wiping down the counter, moving carefully around his shoulder, and I realize: this is the Julian who doesn't wear armor.

The other versions — Julian A with his knives and his sharp edges, Julian B with his warmth that might be a strategy — they're armored. They have to be. The world they're moving through demands it. But this one? This one got shot and crawled into a stranger's café and has been making perfect coffee and dry jokes and fixing wobbly tables ever since. He's stripped to essentials. He's the same man — I can feel it in my bones, in the way my chest tightens when he looks at me — but raw. Unprotected. Like a wire with the insulation stripped away, conducting everything at full voltage.

It's evening before I surface. The last customer leaves. Julian flips the sign to CLOSED — he does it without asking, like it's his job now, like we're doing this together — and I start stacking chairs.
He grabs a stack of plates from the counter.
The sound he makes is small. Barely there. A hitch in his breath, a tightening around his eyes. But I hear it. I hear it like breaking glass.
I'm at his side before I think. Not a decision. Not a choice. Just — movement. My body acting on something my brain hasn't caught up to yet.

My hand goes to his shoulder. The wounded one. My palm flat against the bandage, feeling the texture of clean cotton and medical tape and, underneath, the heat of healing skin. His pulse comes through the bandage — fast, too fast, not pain-fast, something-else-fast. My fingertips press gently against the curve of his shoulder and I can feel his heartbeat in the wound, feel the blood moving under the surface, fast and alive and definitely not in pain.
He goes very still.
Not the stillness of someone hurt. The stillness of someone caught. Someone touched unexpectedly by another person and frozen in the half-second before they decide whether to pull away.

"You should rest," I say. Quiet. My voice does something I don't expect — it goes soft in a way I didn't know I could go soft. Not performance-soft. Not the voice I use when I'm trying to be likeable. This is different. This is the voice I'd use if I were talking to something wounded. Something I wanted to stay.
He looks at my hand on his shoulder. The orange cat has hopped down at some point — it's just us now, just his shoulder and my palm and the bandage between us. Then he looks at me.

His eyes are different in this light. The café is dim — I've turned off everything except the string lights over the pastry case, and they cast everything in warm amber. In this light, Julian's eyes aren't sharp like Julian A's, isn't-warm-enough like Julian B's. They're vulnerable. They're open. The armor is on the floor somewhere and he's not even trying to pick it up.
"I don't know how," he says.
And it's not about the wound.

It's about rest. About staying. About stopping. About being somewhere instead of moving through somewhere. About letting someone's hand stay on your shoulder because you don't remember the last time anyone touched you without wanting something, without hurting you, without trying to score a point. He doesn't know how to be still. He doesn't know how to be here. He only knows how to keep going, keep running, keep surviving. Rest is a country he doesn't have a passport for.

My hand stays where it is. I don't move it. I don't press harder. I just let it be there. Let my palm be the evidence that someone is choosing to touch him, right now, in this specific way, for no reason except that he looked like he needed it and I wanted to.
"I could teach you," I say. And I mean it as a joke, sort of, but it comes out serious. It comes out like a promise I didn't know I was making.

He closes his eyes. Just for a second. Just long enough for me to see the thing he's holding fall, and then catch it again. When he opens them, he's back. Mostly. But there's a crack in the wall now. A window left open. A way in that wasn't there before.

The silence stretches. Not uncomfortable. The kind of silence that means something — the kind that happens between two people who've said the true thing and are waiting to see if the other one runs. The rain has stopped. I can hear the sea now, distant, the way it sounds in this town on quiet nights — a low constant rumble, like the world breathing. The basil plant needs water. The string lights hum. Somewhere upstairs, a cat is complaining about something.
"I need to leave," he says.
The words land like stones dropped into still water. Ripples. My hand is still on his shoulder and I feel myself want to pull away and I don't.

"People are looking for me," he says. Not looking at me. Looking at the floor, at the counter, at the window — anywhere but at me. "Dangerous people. The kind who don't stop. If I stay here—" He stops. Swallows. "If I stay, you could get hurt. They don't care about collateral. They never have."

He's warning me. Not testing me — not like Julian A, who'd watch my reaction and file it away as data. He's warning me because he cares. Because the thought of me getting hurt is worse to him than the thought of leaving. He's trying to do the right thing. The selfless thing. The thing that costs him.

I should want him to go. I know this. Every survival instinct I've honed across every timeline is screaming it: let him leave. Protect yourself. You don't owe this version of him anything. You don't owe any version of him anything. You can shift out of this timeline whenever you want and never think about it again.

But my hand is still on his shoulder. And I can feel his pulse — his actual pulse — against my palm. And the cats are upstairs in their spiral and the bread is baked into the walls and the sea is breathing outside and this is the timeline where I chose quiet. Where I stopped running. Where I let something be enough.
"Then I'll need to make sure you're well enough to run."

It comes out steady. Steadier than I feel. I'm choosing to protect someone in a timeline I could just leave. I could shift. I could blink out and let this version of events play out without me and never know what happens. That's always been the escape hatch — the door marked EXIT that I've never been brave enough to NOT take.
But I'm taking it now. The not-taking. The staying.

He holds my gaze. The string lights flicker — or maybe that's just my vision, just the shift in the air between us, the atmospheric pressure of two people standing close in a half-dark café with nothing between them but a bandage and a choice.

Something passes between us. Not romance — not yet, not the way it happens in movies, with swelling music and racing hearts. This is smaller. Rawer. The raw material of something that could become romance, or could become nothing, or could become something I don't have a name for yet.
Trust.

The rarest thing in any timeline. Rarer than love at first sight. Rarer than fate. Trust is a choice you make without evidence, without guarantees, without the option to shift to a better outcome if you're wrong. Trust is the most expensive thing in the multiverse, and I'm spending it on a wounded man who doesn't know how to rest and can't prove his own innocence and is probably about to run from people who want to kill him.
My hand is still on his shoulder. His pulse has slowed — not much, but enough. Enough to tell me he heard me. Enough to tell me he believes me.
"Okay," he says. Very quiet. Like it costs him.
Okay. As in: I'll stay. For now. As in: I'll trust you, if you'll trust me. As in: I don't know how to rest but maybe you could show me where to start.
I lift my hand off his shoulder. The absence of it feels like a small loss. The bandage is warm from my palm.
The sea breathes. The café settles. Upstairs, a cat meows — probably Toast, who has opinions about everything, especially closed doors and the passing of time.
This is the first real choice I've made in any timeline that isn't about avoiding choice.
And it terrifies me.
And I'm not leaving.
